captions: VISITA A LA S.I. CATEDRAL DE ZAMORA Y MUSEO
pages.text:
Thursday July 31st 2008            Joan swimming pool
                                   Charles says ok **
We slept in __Zamora campsite__ + drove into
Zamora the next day, parking across the
river. The Cathedral has an amazing, fragile-
looking dome. Simon, Eve & Charles (19) are in
Spain. We arrived here by ferry with Thomas, Nadin
+ Austin who have gone over the Pyrenees in their
green camping van. We are looking at
Wellington's Battle Fields before joining them at
David's house.
   The tapestries in the cathedral museum
were fresh-looking with bright colours. The
misericords had been nailed down by a
bashful bishop because they showed lewd
monks. The present bishop made sure we
didn't get to see them, either, by roping
them off.

captions: San Pedro de la Nave
pages.text: We had lunch in town, pork chops, & then drove about 17 miles to S. Pedro, a C- & church, Quite like St. Peter's in Cambridge. It has the crispest Romanesque capitols that I've ever seen - Daniel in the Den + Isaac sacrificing Esau. It had been moved up, stone by stone, to a higher village in 1930's when they flooded the valleys. Also flooded were the ravines which Wellington crossed

when he evaded the French Blockade by going through rough country NW of Zamora. He was pulled across the ravines in a hammock.
	We returned to Zamora & had supper on benches on a little platform overlooking the river. An amazing number of Storks flew past & there were 25 on the finials of the cathedral & the tower.
	At 8.30 we joined a group to look at the Co olive mills, 3 of them, by the river. They've restored the mill wheel & hammer very well.
__Friday August 1st!__
	We set off for Salamanca & parked across the river. We saw the Patio de Escuelas, with the amazing plateresque carved front to the university building, with the symbol for lust - a frog on a skull (see over). We saw the house of shells, & inside it to a lovely courtyard. The old & new cathedrals are beautiful - domes & 1492 [illegible] [illegible]

captions: Plateresque at Salamanca University
pages.text:
[Photo/postcard of Salamanca — caption written vertically: Plateresque at Salamanca University]

Charles is amused that ~~Simon~~ Eve is struggling in Italian while Simon pulls out the French.
	We had tapas for lunch which were delicious. Deep-fried shrimp + a fancy thing with beers. Charles, who doesn't like fish

had a pizza.
	We have arrived at an extraordinary campsite __Camping Torres__ by a lake near Salamanca. Everything that can be wrong is wrong + unwelcoming. It reminds Simon of Fawlty Towers. The owners told us to return at 7pm + go to reception to pay. They weren't there. The older man kept pacing up + down watching his 3 year old son? grandson? practising for the Tour de France + finally, when we had sat down for supper, came up + demanded to see our passports. Simon said he'd come after supper. Then the man told Charles to move his washing line, because people might be put off coming because of it. There are only 2 other families here. They have been watering the grass constantly since we arrived. We only saw the sign because we pulled off the road. __CAMP NO[illegible]__
It says:
so no wonder there are so few people.
The washing machine is still on its

packaging (although it's shown in the book as providing a machine.) The taps don't work for providing water for the van. The shower tap took ages to run warm (I counted to 70, a conventional tap) & the foot tray blocked. They were most un welcoming when we arrived.
__Saturday__
   The sprinklers were still on when we left in the morning. We drove to Arapile & Simon & Eve walked up a ridge to where Wellington stood, watching & where he had his glass. There was a gap in the French column, so he sprang an attack & decimated 40,000 French soldiers in 40 minutes. It was the beginning of the end of Napoleon's Europe. They were fighting in 1812, July 21st, only a fortnight before us. The hills are baking hot, the nights cold. No tents were issued until 1813. At the end of the battle, the stubble caught fire & burned up the wounded. Wellington had 52,000 men. The French numbered 50,000. Wellington found a good place to watch from. (He loved riding, whereas his Spanish counterpart, having once

captions: Spanish Guerrilla
pages.text:
been run over by his cavalry, travelled in a cart drawn by 8 mules.) Wellington hid his men behind the ridge.
——— line (used by English)
————→ column (used by French)
[sketch of concentric squares] Square
Spanish Guerrilla

[Printed brochure page glued in — no handwritten diary text. Printed headings and body text from a museum guide: FRENCH DOLLS; ART DECO GLASS (mentions René Lalique, Ernest Sabino); NANCY SCHOOL GLASS (mentions Emile Gallé, Daum brothers, Majorelle, Legra, Paul Nicolas); PERFUME BOTTLES (mentions René Lalique, Coty, Houbigant, Worth, Guerlain, Gallé, Daum brothers, Baccarat, Saint Louis). Photos of a bisque-headed doll, Art Deco glass vase, amber lamp, and green perfume bottle.]

places: Museum of Art Nouveau + Art Deco
pages.text:
We drove to the end of the Roman Bridge at Salamanca again + walked across + up into the Museum of Art Nouveau + Art Deco in the Casa de Lis. The perfume bottles are lovely the dolls. We spent quite a time discussing them.
We had lunch on the pavement + drove to a le Clerk super market.

captions: ÁVILA (postcard, labelled "PATRIMONIO DE LA HUMANIDAD")
pages.text: On we went to Avila. The heat caught up with us, so we didn't climb the walls. We saw the cathedral. It has a beautiful alabaster altar, but it is roped off so you can't see it. There were more nice alabaster carvings in the musean which we could see.

We drove on to a campsite which is as well run as the last one was badly run. The man at the accuil wasn't very good at communicating + didn't finish interviews properly. He just expected people to realise they should go + look for their own site. He gave me the key to the ladies' disable toilet, where the washing machine was. I did a wash + we went for a swim in the lovely [?lake]. Our pitch is near the chemical toilet with a small gate leading out to a rocky path + down to the lake.

    The camp is packed cheek-by-jowl. People from Madrid with their microwaves + fridges in rented caravans. Cigarette smoke + [strikethrough] rock music + computer games. Very friendly + there's a baby Justin's age nearby, with his toddler brother [strikethrough: that] whom I've pushed on his bike. No shade, so Simon's put up the awning. He's had to mend the water tank strap that ripped off on its way down here,

the fridge + the clock. Lots of flies.
	The owner (55ish) is a retired pharmacist ~~who~~ from Madrid who wanted a life-change + bought 2 campsites.
Sunday
	We had a leisurely morning. Simon + Eve swam in the lake. We swam 3 times in the day. On the final swim I went to an outcrop of rocks on the far side of the lake. It was one of the best swims of my life. The campsite was unbearably hot, so we took deckchairs down to a pine grove by the lake where it was cooler.
	By late afternoon, most of the camp-site had emptied + the bar had closed so we couldn't get chips for supper. There was barely enough bread for breakfast.
	Across the lake is a bird area - 14 nesting pairs of black vultures. I may have seen one while swimming across.

We saw a white-headed eagle, we think, as we left Avila. There are lots of sparrows.
	This campsite I would give ***
Charles (too hot + crowded) *

Simon	* * *.
Although it's crowded, it's clean + the lake is marvellous. The owner has another campsite in the area.
__Monday 5th August__
We drove round the lake (+ lots of zig zags) to look at the other campsite, but it looked gloomy + there was no lake to swim in.
We reached Toledo + stayed at the Cardinal Hostal, a really lovely hotel, an old house belonging to a cardinal, wood, metal, tiles. Marvellous air con. Outside it was 40°. Sweat poured.

captions: TOLEDO
pages.text:
The river goes round W S + E & to the north, where our hotel is, it a V. steep hill.

Fortunately just near our hotel, is a huge escalator, which carries you up a large chunk of the hill. Definitely a plus! The hotel had a very good restaurant in the court yard.

__Tuesday__ We saw 2 synagogues (one of which did a bag search) + a Vizigothic Museum, El Grecos (his house shut) & the cathedral. Marvellous misericordes - a woman having a bath in a barrel with a shower curtain.
	 I really like Toledo. The streets are higgledy piggledy.
__Wednesday__
We drove on to Madrid. Susana + Eugenio were most welcoming + we stayed in their beautiful flat. Simon re parked the van, where we later got into trouble with a blue/green parking ticket. We walked to the Prado where they showed us their favourite pictures. Eugenio told us some of the history. We walked on to lunch + ate a fabulous meal. Rissoles, lamb chops, black pudding + doryfish baked in rock salt - piquant + crunchy. The puddings were good too - crepe cooked with lemon & leaves inside.

captions: The [?Family] of Carlos [?III] [?Madrid] 1800 Goya. Prado Blue boy on left in the [?] a felon (betrayed his people) Most beautifully painted [?dress] in the middle
pages.text: The [?Family] of Carlos [?III] / [?Madrid] 1800 / Goya. Prado / Blue boy on left in the [?] / a felon / (betrayed his people) / Most beautifully painted [?dress] in the middle

We caught a taxi back + had a siesta. Then Simon + Charles + Eugenio chatted. Suzana + Eve walked back to the centre - Puerta del Sol - + bought a Spanish dress for Flora with hairslide, fan + castanettes + a Spanish apron for Lizzie. Only at 10.45. did we go out for a walk + just before midnight we went to a bar, to give Charles (+ us!) a bowl of gazpacho + a sort of omlette! Suzana + Eugenio say they wouldn't normally have eaten again. It's still incredibly hot - they say hotter than usual - 40° in Toledo, but only 37° in Madrid. We have fans in the bedrooms.

__Thursday__
We left as the parking metre started at 9 am. Elevenses was an ideal spot, at the edge of a field of sunflowers under the shade of a willow tree. Charles paddled in the river but didn't fall in. It was so cool that he wore a sweater. We shopped at a super market for lunch.

captions: Susana Triana likes this artist Zurbaran still life with 4 vases 1658-64. Prado
El Caldero where we ate
pages.text:
Susana Triana likes this artist Zurbaran still life with 4 vases 1658-64. Prado

El Caldero where we ate

places: Embalse de la Cuerta del Pozo
pages.text:
It took ages to find the campsite, circling round the lakes. We are near the source of the R. Aouro, [strikethrough]near[/strikethrough] __El Frontal__ ****
near the Embalse de la Cuerta del Pozo. in the hills of Orbion. It promised a washing machine, but there isn't one. So I did a big handwash + then I swam (Simon + Charles paddled) in the enormous lake. There's a huge expanse of land under pine trees, but not that many campers. Graffiti + broken handles + locks in the loos. A lovely campsite.
__Friday__
	We had a restful day. Simon + Eve walked 2 miles to a bar along the lake where they rent out boats, but there were only canoes, no windsurfer.
	In the afternoon we swam. The campsite is underused. Showers I had were luke warm, the door handles broken + doors had graffiti. It's run by gov't agriculture dept. But the setting + the lake are beautiful.
__Saturday__
We left + motored around the hills to see another campsite, no lake, more crowded.

On over hilly country, skiing. We visited the Laguna Negra. Someone has written a poem about it. Really lovely. In over the pass of Puerta de Santa Inés, which is closed in winter. Down the other side we have come to a pretty place for lunch with a fast-flowing river, There are huge cliffs to the west, warm in the sunshine! Counted 14 huge birds circling. 6 or more ft. wing span. Eagles? It was amazing.

     We came out of the hills onto a plain with vines + pear trees, the main area for Rioc Rioja. Windmills + photo voltaic cells. We stopped at St. Maria Eurate (again) on the pilgrimage route. There's a stone colonnade around it.

     We reached Camping Ezcaba [illegible] north of [?A]Pamplona. It's near Suaren, where Wellington fought. It's about the best campsite we've been in. At reception 2 women chatted for ages ignoring us, very rudely. It was already

5.40. + we were in a great hurry to get the bedding into a washing machine. The last 2 campsites that advertised a washing machine didn't have one. This had 2, + they were both free + we managed to wash + dry the bedding before night. We also swam in a beautiful pool. The [illegible — obscured by tape] showers were heated by solar panels (only they didn't always work!) There were 69 steps up from the lower terrace where we were to the "[?welco-]

[Right column, partially obscured by tape:]  We had [illegible] the camp[site] restaurant. Charles + Eve had pizza. Simon had ½ rabbit. At 11.45. a group of children came out noisily to our [?terrace]. Charles wandered out to the loo in his Boxers. A very cross French camper shouted "Silence!"

__Sunday 10th August__
     We drove to look at the Bridge at Sorauren where Wellington wrote to ask for reinforcements - Paet's group. Very very hilly.
     Then on to see Roncesvalles.

We drove through Roncevalles, + the the town, because there were no parking places, but we stopped at the pass which had beautiful views. The police were checking for terrorist bombs (ETA etc).
   We arrived at a marvellous campsite, __Cave de Sauveterre de Bearn__ ***** friendly + a lovely river to ~~uto~~ wade through + swim in. Charles made the wading look so easy. We each swam + walked. It was much easier to get through the paper work in a French campsite + nice French squatter loos. We were a bit low on treats for supper + a nice English family - a man + woman from Poole - gave us a frozen Mars Bar. A woman cyclist from England has won a medal in the Beijing Olympics. There was a thunderstorm in the night. Rain in the morning. We drove up into the town to buy bread + have breakfast. Simon + Eve had walked round the town the evening before.
   There's a Romanesque church with an old Nativity capital + a capital of the gourmond + the beggar.

__Monday__ After breakfast in the town square, watching canoeists assemble, we drove on to a supermarket. The breadshop provide so much business for village centres.
   We shopped in a supermarket most of the morning + reached __Queyssac__ campsite, ***** 7k north of Bergerac, by evening, Charles' favourite campsite. It was open, good views, a lake with ducks. Simon made tea for 3 English women in a tent next door. It rained, for the first time on this trip. Really good showers.

__Tuesday__
We started breakfast out doors + moved in because of the rain. We drove off north towards David's house. We lunched by a river in the rain + then went to the Tree Museum at Chamberet. Bees were carrying an ant out of their [?observation hive]. We arrived at [?Sard's] house, [?Sas Noux], at 4, just after Thomas + [illegible] Naomi + Austin. Dylan (17) had had 6 friends to stay + had managed to tidy up the house well.

Alan + Jean + Sally + David made us very welcome. The house now has a staircase + upstairs bathroom. David + Dylan have made a patio. (But it was too cold to sit there. We chatted + chatted in the kitchen. For the first night David cooked us, steak with tomato + roquefort sauce à la Dylan.
__Wednesday__ The next night Charles put in 2 chickens while the rest of us were on a super 12 mile walk, with a beer at St. Hilaire. The house is very comfortable now.
__Thursday__
	Thomas played is viola, Dylan played his recorder. Then Jem played his guitar. Simon + Eve + Charles left. We stopped in Maymac at + shopped at a good green grocer. Charles found a field for us to have lunch. It started raining as I chopped the salad so we finished in the van.

I'm sitting in a blanket while I write this. It's so cold. We reached __St. Jean de Vaux__ where it continued to feel very cold, so we didn't swim. The grass was ominously green (lots of rain.)
	For the first time the restaurant in the village was open, so we had a meal Café du Centre. Charles had steak, Simon pintade (game bird) + I had garotted chicken. Very good food. We had bought puddings earlier in the day + went back to the van to eat them.
	We also tasted wine for William's + Bryony's wedding.
__Friday__
We picked up the 60 bottles of wine, at 9.30, + some others + set off northwards. We shopped + drove on until 6, arriving at an almost deserted campsite with one other van. It's a bit warmer.
__Ribemont__ **

captions: Peckover House, Cambridgeshire, The Drawing Room
pages.text:
April 4th 2009
Simon & Eve & Tonic drove over to [?Trumpby] & took Helen & Margaret out for a picnic lunch at Hampton ? Lode, by the

R. Severn. We could see the steam train across the water. Margaret was strong enough to walk quite a long way across the field & back.

__Saturday April 18th.__
Simon, Eve & Tonic set off eastwards. A[?t] was chilly, but gradually the sun broke through. We had lunch in the carpark near Peckover House, a very nice Georgian NT house. Built 1722 & lived in from 1796 by the Peckham family, Quaker bankers. We joined a tour of the garden. The Tulip tree has flat, -[?gottahed] leaves. There's also a quince tree & a ginks tree. Originated 65 mill. years ago (fossils found in coal seams). Really a [strikethrough: ten] fern. Within 4 months ginkos were found sprouting in the ruins of Hiroshima in 1944.

captions: The church of St. Mary + All Saints, Fotheringay!
pages.text:
Earlier in the day we had passed through Fotheringay, the village where Mary Queen of Scots was held captive. The Castle is no longer there, but the church is very impressive. There

[photo: illuminated church at night]

The church of St. Mary + All Saints, Fotheringay!

was [struck: at] a very chatty woman with a black dog.

****

__The Queen's Head__, __Foulsham__, where we are staying, has 5 pigs in a paddock at the end of the pub garden/ campsite. Tonic is bemused. [marginal annotation: lamb+mint / [?sct] pud.]

Horror of horrors! I had forgotten to bring extra loo paper. The cornershop where we stopped in the depths of Norfolk was manned by an Indian (via Kenya)

__Sunday__

Why is it that country people feel that they can be as rude as they like at the mention of "Birmingham?" Apart from that, the church, Holy Innocents, was very friendly. Children were at the Baptist church.

On to lunch in the carpark at Blakeney, which was by the sea according to the map, but infact we could [struck] scarcely see it because of mud flats. We walked but it was windy. Lots of birdspotters. In the end we drove inland – less traffic &

more sun. Small zigzag roads to Seapalling.

	Lots of ex-windmills.

We found a pleasant small campsite on The old bridge road in Potter Heigham. After opening the campingvan curtains in the afternoon we saw a group of Dutch campers who had come on a course about making rammed earth houses.

	In the evening we visited a boat yard on the river Yare. A company Sheerline who make river boats took a chad catalogue. We also went to a rural business park that was well designed and dropped catalogues at two interior design firms. It was then an hour to Halesworth, but we stopped just north at Spexhall and walked through the churchyard, across a field and stream up to a farm and back. Tonic showed a return of youthful energy. After lunch we picked up lampshades from WK Howarth and set off for Annette, briefly stopping to drop another catalogue on the way.

	Annette gave us tea in the garden in pleasant sunshine. We returned to 86 at about 7.45. Charles had already eate.

Mayors Sunday
At 9.50, we went to church & then on to Enville for lunch in a field. (S.E, Tonic)
	At 3.45 we arrived at Pat Gregory's. Her sister, Barbara, visited too from 5 doors up the road. Lovely hills visible from the house, Caer Caradoc, Church Stretton. 3 kinds of cake! Beautiful stream & garden.

	On to Hamperley campsite, SE corner Long Mynd. We walked before bed. It was warm in the sun. 2 families had b.b.q.s. Log fire in the woods. Games — football & frisbee in the field. Large flock of sheep.

	Rain in the morning & we got ~~stuck~~ stuck again! 15 years on from the first time. 6 Sea Cadets from Kiddel & Kingswi[?] came straight over, 5 men & one woman, & pushed us out. We had breakfast by the gate.

	On to Oswestry by a route to the west partly in Wales

We arrived at the [?grange] + Simon's Mary welcomed us with coffee. We drove off + looked at the little church. lovely kneelers. They had given 2 cows to Africa for the Millennium. We had lunch at the Hand. Then Simon took us on a very strenuous walk up a vertical hill. It's very pretty there. Back in Oswestry we visited the church + church centre. Pat Gregory's father was church warden in the 1970's + his name is there. Mary cooked us a delicious supper + we came home.

        May 17th Sunday.
  2pm we set off in the rain to take Margaret + Helen out. to tea.
        We turned off the main road at Bewdley + drove along a very narrow lane to sit in a field with nice wooden shacks round the edge of it. The train went by a few times. It rained + it rained. Then we took Margaret + Helen back to Trimpley + went home.

May 31st Sunday
a glorious afternoon. We took Helen + Margaret out to tea. Hampton Lode was full of traffic + traffic jams, so Simon drove towards Arley, found a field - the same view as from the Trimpley summerhouse.

        __Saturday July 18th__
        We took Grandma + Helen out to [?&] lunch again on tiny roads near Trimpley

        __Friday July 24th__
John, Simon, Eve + Tonic went along beyond Kings Norton, right before Wythall along Icknield St. On the way Simon showed John where he caught the bus + grew up at Kings Norton, Fairenoad Rise.
        We parked by a gate for supper, looking at a view of sheep in a field. We couldn't get the van off the road to walk. It was 9.15, so we came home.

captions: Traditional White Gingerbread
pages.text:
Thursday August 7  070809
Friday 11.15, Simon, Eve & Tonic set off to Northumberland. Tonic's ill.

We stopped to admire the Humber Bridge + walked down to the water's edge through a nature park near an old black windmill. A man arrived in a car & collected sea weed.
      On [the] to Bridlington.
      Pradhma, Godfrey, Emy & Evans welcomed us warmly + picked apples from the tree in their garden + gave us a good supper. It was lovely to see them.
      We drove on + camped at __Muston Grange Caravan Park__, extraordinarily regimented & densely packed caravans. £12.50. Nice people.
      We have had a [?re] series of spills on this trip. I knocked over the dog water bowl. Simon lifted a half-bottle of cider by the stopper & it exploded over the floor. Pradhma tipped water over me at supper. (At lunch today Simon shook the French dressing & it sprayed out of the 20 year old bottle which has developed a split.)
      Worst of all, Tonic had bits of

watery diahorrea all day. He was restless in the night. I heard a watery fart & leapt out of bed to drag open the door & found I was treading in a trail of shine with one foot. It was very dark but I could see it gleaning up towards the door. Simon woke up. Later he said I appeared paralysed. It was truly ghastly. We threshed about with mats & buckets of water & soap.... One of my worst camping nights.
[Friday] Saturday
 After breakfast we attempted damage limitation on Tonic's brown puddles, pouring water on slurry round the camp site.
Then off under glorious sunshine to Whitby. Scarborough looks very fine (no evidence of Butlins) beautiful Edwardian houses. Whitby was peaceful green, green, yellow stone & a blue sea & sky. We

met a nice woman Episcopal Priest from Pennsylvania who remembered about the bird flying through the hall.
We have stopped for lunch on the road towards Teesside.      26 [?Coalhouses]? [illegible] no [illegible],
When we reached the campsite we went for a walk. Tonic had some eggs for supper & felt better. The walk was at first over stiles. Simon decided to take a "short cut" but we couldn't get out of the barbed wire/ditches surrounding the field & ended up walking 4 sides of it. An uneventful night, thank goodness.

Sunday
 We arrived at the Alnick Gardens at 9.30. & got about the only car park slot in the shade, which was just as well for Tonic as the Gardens don't allow dogs. He's still not well. Until 1pm we visited the gardens, having a tour of the Poison Garden & looking at the Grand Cascade, which Simon

captions: boy standing in water sculpture (great anticipation when fountains start)
girl paddling in ornamental [?gdns]
treehouse—
pages.text:
① boy standing in water sculpture (great anticipation when fountains start)
② girl paddling in ornamental [?gdns]
③ treehouse—
__walk way develops into swing bridges . Restaurants.__
thought was too fussy. The sculptures are fantastic, especially the vortex. Bamboo [illegible] maze . The children drove toy dumper trucks & are encouraged to splash in the fountains.

You need to take changes of clothes. It really is a delight for all ages.
	We had lunch in the van & drove north to Bamburgh. We went to & said evensong at St. Aidan's. We're camping at __Budle Farm Campsite__. The farmer's very nice, He's given us the vet's number for tomorrow, some of his calves' dioralyte for this evening. Tonic doesn't like drinking it. We walked along the beach at high tide & I collected shells. It's very beautiful & looks across to Holy Island.
Simon has brought gin & Tonic on this trip. It makes it even nicer.

__Monday__ boring rain!
	We went to Woolmer for the vet. Tonic's now doped up & recovering. He even walked to __Dunstanborough__, tugging on the lead. After the vet we crossed to Holy Island/Lindisfarne. The castle's shut on Mondays, so we looked at the old Priory. It was orginally set up, economically, to support 10 monks on revenue from surrounding villages. But by the time of the Dissolution there were

captions: Dunstanburgh Castle. Early C4. Lancastrian Stronghold during Wars of the Roses. Built by a nobleman to show off to the king.
pages.text:
only a prior, 2 monks + lots of servants. We drove on to Craster + parked + walked to Dunstanborough + back - glorious sunshine. We've come back to the same lovely little campsite, Budle farm.

[photo of Dunstanburgh Castle from aerial view]

Dunstanburgh Castle. Early C4. Lancastrian Stronghold during Wars of the Roses. Built by a nobleman to show off to the king.

__Tuesday__
   We returned to Lindisfarne to see the Castle, hoping to see the Castle in the morning & then go on to Cragside in the afternoon. However the Castle didn't open until 12. So we packed a lunch + walked around the island, seeing Jekell's beautiful garden. We arrived at the castle at 12 but there was a huge queue, so we decided to return in winter.
   When we reached Cragside, near Rothbury, there was again a huge queue to get into the house. So we saw the Iron Bridge & lakes & Pumphouse & Simon walked to the Powerhouse where Lord Armstrong made hydro electric power. Now we have come on to __Snitter Farm__ __Campsite__, an open view of woods & fields. Tonic is getting better, gradually allowed to eat more but dreadful smells
   Simon & I are sitting in a howling gale + weeping with laughter because his farts are so bad.

__Wednesday__

We had originally intended to go back to Cragside but NT properties are so crowded in August that instead we went west to __Kielder Water__ & walked by the reservoir. It would make a good cycling holiday [drawing] to rent a cottage here & cycle around [drawing] with young children. The path appears to go right round (50m?) + a ferry would take you to 5 jetties around the lake.
Going on southwards, we crossed between Weardale & Teesdale, through Stanhope B6278, glorious empty moorland. We reached Bower & Peter & Elizabeth gave us a lovely supper. We slept in the nearby car park.

__Thursday__

Simon discussed garden projects. At midday we left for a walk & lunch at Arkengarthdale. After lunch in the van, Peter led us on a super walk, up quite a steep hillside where the track disappeared under a slag heap. Lovely wild flowers in meadows by the river & little bridges. We risked the "bull in field sign".

captions: Kielder Water
pages.text: We left for Bham at 4pm + had [~~lunch at~~] supper at Pinnocchio's When we got home.

captions: Looking up the Punch Bowl, Winsford Hill. We walked around this.
pages.text:
__September/October.__ We have taken Margaret + helen out for 3 teas/lunches in the van.

__October 16th Friday.__
1.50. Simon, Eve & Tonic left for a w/end holiday. Bright sunshine on the M5 down past Bristol. We arrived at 5.40. at __Halse Farm campsite__ * * * * (middle of Exmoor) It's nice not having to wait until 4.10. to leave, when the children finish school. This is a working farm - cows, sheep, chicken + 2 barking sheep dogs. The farmer has boarded over the tractor wheels flush with the tyre, to keep mud out.

__Saturday__
A cold frost in the night. I had to get up twice to put more clothes on. We went on a 4 3/4 mile walk along Winsford Hill, down to Winsford village. Friendly P.O. good lunch. Walk back up the hill - one of the nicest I've been on, along a stream & up a very, very old track. No steep climbing. Tea. Now we're sitting in the sun with the van door open.

captions: Looking up the Punch Bowl, Winsford Hill. We walked around this.
Winsford Village - we walked through this, lots of bridges.
The Royal Oak Inn, Winsford where we had lunch - the peanut butter ice cream
pages.text:
Looking up the Punch Bowl, Winsford Hill. We walked around this.

Winsford Village - we walked through this, lots of bridges.

The Royal Oak Inn, Winsford where we had lunch - the peanut butter ice cream

On the walk across the moorland we saw stacks of large hay bales, but when we got up close we saw that they had, in fact, baled the local vegetation - bracken + heather. It would make marvellous walls for sheep shelters? Edible?
	Simon had gone out to look at the stars which were v. bright on the friday night, however on the Saturday night the clouds came.
__Sunday__ The night was very cold. We wore hats + socks. I got too hot in the night + took of my jeans + socks + then regretted it.
	We had switched to red gas - propane - but it ran out (the same can that we had had all along) + the blue gas can barely work. Fortunately the heater stayed on while I got dressed. It was cold at breakfast + I couldn't cook toast.
	On the journey we stopped at to look at an engine house in the Brandon Hills. It hauled a cable for a train up to the mines. This was the top of the cable [illegible]. The old [?Gindling] had metal bars sticking out of the floor for

anchoring the cables. There were chimneys showing where furnaces had been for providing steam power. We didn't have time to go down the path + explore where the cable went.
	At 12.50. we arrived at the Thatch, Queens Camel, for lunch with the Urbaincyks. Eve had mis-map read a bit. We had a delicious lunch + the house is very pretty.
__Saturday October 24th.__
Simon + Eve take Margaret + Helen out to tea.

__Friday April 9th 2010__
@ 6.15. Simon + Eve set off with Tonic post letter, reverse, fetch ½ bottle wine + ½ bottle of milk of kitchen counter, 6.20. set off again. £12 ***
	We drove thru [?Sunshine] to __the Walks__ Penrhos, Wales, on the way to the Brecons 8.05. good journey. Lovely plain with

distant hills all round. Daffodils (late spring) 3 graves in middle of camp site, house wi. shutters, pond, bridge, Welsh flag, clean loo.
  __April 10th__. Mummy's + Daddy's 81st wedding anniversary.
  Gorgeous sunshine. Relaxed. On the road by 9.15.
  We parked + had coffee + set off with lunch on a beautiful walk at about 10.45 from Torpantau where the Brecon to Merthyr railway emerged from a tunnel. We followed a forestry road down to the river where we crossed to the west bank and walked north parallel to the river until the Lower Neuadd Reservoir.  We crossed the dam after having lunch in a sunny spot and turned north again up the old "Roman Road" to the pass overlooking Brecon.  Eve thought Tonic had walked enough so carried him back from here in the backpack.  It was a marvellous

walk + we caught some sun.**
  As we neared __Crickhowell__ I telephoned Joan Clarke, now 92, + we went round to see her taking supper.  Briefly, in the hall, we met her niece, Jane, who was on her way out to supper.  Joan seems very happy where she lives Flat 21 Maes y Ffynnon, Beaufort St. 01873-810664.  (campsite £15, expensive + extra charge for showers)

__Sunday April 11th__.
  Another sunny morning.  We ate up half breakfast, walked up to St. Edmund's church where we heard about the re-setting-up of a credit union + then walked down for the other half of breakfast.  Tonic + I also went to see the dog walk by the river.
  10.30. we set off.
  We visited Abbey Dore.  Four young men in one family died in WW2 in the RAF + their 16 year old [?sister] died in 1936, according to one of the [illegible] graves outside.  We had a lunch in the pub carvery where they are polite but the food was pretty dire.
  We called in at Trampley on the way home + had tea in the summer house.

Friday May 7th.

6.25t we left Westfield [?S] & drove to Moreton on the Marsh, Simon had forgotten the map so we didn't find out original campsite near Blockley. We ended up arriving too late (8.10) & slept in carpark [illegible].

__Saturday May 8__th

Cold, miserable weather. By 8.30 we were having breakfast at William's & Bryony's B&B in Lower Swell, Cotswolds. Very fancy B&B, so posh Simon had to repark the van. Old vine in the conservatory.
By 10 we were at __Pangolin Foundry__ in Chalford. Andy, the designer had organised for a group of students to visit & find out about mould-making & casting metal sculptures - hand sized giants (Bull in Bull ring). Alloys - bronze. (Cornley's are cast in iron, industrially, so he doesn't use them) lots of work for [?Admira] [?Hirst]. 92 people work there. Very interesting. Lost wax. Sand moulds [?or] with resin, injected with CO₂. Sue, the designer, asked lots of questions.
     We had lunch in the van, then rested. At 4.40, W&B picked us up in their super car & drove us to Winchcombe to David Thistlethwaite's

& Sarah Loveday's exhibition of paintings. It was good to see David & Ali after so many years.
     We went out to supper at the Fox, lovely walnut & honey tart. Then back to our campsite, __Folly Farm__, **** £12 where we're parked under beautiful apple blossom.

__Sunday May 9__th

     Slightly better weather today. We again breakfasted with William & Bryony. The landlady's husband had been an architect for townhalls. We think he bought the old rectory farm & then found the foundations of out buildings & managed to build about 3 more Cotswold houses in the [?old] garden: Glebe House, Barn Cottage etc.
     We set off to Snowshill Manor, having a bit of trouble with a cycle race enroute. The garden to Snowshill Manor is the most lovely I've seen, on the side of a hill, compartments. Benches, ponds, barns. Model village, old fire engines. Charles Wade's collection of 22,000 objects grew so large that he stored it in [?&] the Manor house & moved, with his wife, into the priest's

captions: Snows-hill Manor Glouos [?Selt] South Front
pages.text:
[photo of Snowshill Manor, south front]

Snows-
hill
Manor

Glouos

[?Selt]
South
Front

house behind it, where he installed electricity &
hot water. He often sat there next to the fire
in a hall porter's chair. A rope was next to

him & when he pulled it, it worked the
bellows in the next room. Bryony & I enjoyed
looking at the dolls' houses. Simon &
looked at the warping? machine, large,
6ft high, 6ft diametre, for # wrapping
silk thread around at the correct tension
for the weaver. Lots of spinning wheels.
   Wagons from different countries, ships,
clocks, walking frames for toddlers, one
for round a post (on a ship?) Crafts,
colours, useful, beautiful things. One of
the best places I've been.
   We had lunch in the carpark. They
drove back to London & we are going
back to Birmingham.
   __23 May Sunday__
   Traffic jams as we left [?Sham] at 1.30
to take Margaret & Helen out for a
Strawberry Tea. A lovely sunny
afternoon.

captions: Music room, Snowshill Manor. There is also a child's harp, drums, recorder + a Serpent, covered in leather.
pages.text: Music room, Snowshill Manor. There is also a child's harp, drums, recorder + a Serpent, covered in leather.

Friday June 11th.
	At 2.15. we left for Wales. Simon, Eve, Peter, Magda Lipińska. And Tonic.
	We drove to Ludlow. There were pigeons nesting next to the loos in the Castle. They were setting up a stage for Othello. Then, after much trouble, we found a bar serving tea. The tea shops shut at 4.45!
	Charles is also with us in the blue car. He found the way to the campsite + telephoned us when we took the wrong turning on the way to the campsite. *** 2 loos + 2 showers.
	__Elstich Barns__ campsite -
showers.
	There's a covered road off it where Peter + Magda walked, but supper wasn't ready when they [strikethrough: out] got back at [strikethrough: 8] 7.30. We drank Magda's present - Zubrovka made out of bison grass. Delicious. Deceptively easy to drink! Lovely local straw berries.
	The farmer came to visit, bringing an enormous old wasps' nest in a 'black sack' that he'd just removed from his roof. Lovely views.

pages.sketch_desc: Pencil sketch at top of page showing a landscape view of rolling hills/mountains, with some tree/vegetation outlines, labelled as the view from the campsite at Eastward, Shropshire.
pages.text:
Saturday June 12th

View from table + chairs behind hedge at camp site Eastward, Shropshire.
we need to buy, bread milk tea glue cafetiere

    We went to Stokesay Castle + each listened to a tape recording of its history as we wandered around. A beautiful place. It surrendered to Cromwell's troops in the civil war. Lawrence of Ludlow had permission to build a castle from the king. It wasn't one of the Marches Castles.
Enroute to the Elan Valley Reservoirs we had an outdoor lunch at a pub. There were 3 or more Reservoirs, built 1886-19[illegible]. At any time they had 5,000 men working on them, 12,000 men were on the books. A final reservoir was opened by Queen Elizabeth, her first appointment on ascending

captions: Stokesay Castle 1280 fortified manor house
pages.text:
Stokesay Castle
1280 fortified
manor house
